description: Consciousness science - theories, neural correlates, hard problem.

facts.context: The hard problem of consciousness asks:
facts.truth: why subjective experience exists at all
distractors: where consciousness is localized in brain

facts.context: Integrated Information Theory (IIT) measures consciousness via:
facts.truth: phi, the amount of irreducible integrated information
distractors: neuron count in the brain structure

facts.truth: minimal brain activity sufficient for experience
distractors: entire brain required for any consciousness

facts.truth: fundamental property present in all matter
distractors: only present in biological neural networks
an illusion with no objective reality
emergent only in sufficiently complex systems

facts.context: Global Workspace Theory explains consciousness as:
facts.truth: information broadcast widely across brain regions
distractors: localized processing in single brain area

facts.context: The binding problem asks how the brain:
facts.truth: unifies separate features into coherent experience

facts.context: Split-brain studies suggest consciousness may be:
facts.truth: divisible with separate awareness in each hemisphere
distractors: always unified regardless of brain structure
located only in the left hemisphere

facts.context: Higher-order theories require consciousness to involve:
facts.truth: mental states representing other mental states

facts.context: Philosophical zombies are hypothetical beings that:
facts.truth: behave identically to humans but lack experience
distractors: have experience but cannot behave normally

facts.context: Anesthesia studies inform consciousness research by:
facts.truth: revealing which brain changes eliminate awareness
distractors: proving consciousness is located in one region
showing consciousness cannot be studied scientifically
having no relevance to consciousness research

facts.context: Predictive processing theories view consciousness as:
